coredata.dc:title: Enhancing proton conduction of high temperature proton exchange membranes based on carbon dots doped polyvinyl chloride nanofibers
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ke
Wei, Xiaoqing
Hu, Shu
Li, Qingquan
Gao, Weimin
Wu, Dan
Che, Quantong
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) as the easy-to-get and cheap carbon nanomaterials exhibited the great potential in various high-performance electrolytes. In this research, we constructed high temperature proton exchange membranes with multilayered microstructures through a couple of polyvinyl chloride (PVC) nanofibers layers wrapping a thin CDs layer. In the prepared (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane, the CDs provided a mount of sites to anchor phosphoric acid (PA) molecules with the formation of the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane. The proton conduction was accelerated by the continuous proton conduction channels consisting of the CDs layer and PA molecular chains. Notably, the proton conduction behavior was guided by the PVC nanofibers in the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane. Furthermore, a large number of hydrophilic oxygenated functional groups surrounding CDs facilitated the proton conduction process owing to the reduced proton conduction resistance in the hydrophilic membrane. For the PVC/ImCDs/PA membrane, the imidazolium groups could enhance proton conductivity. From our perspective, the imidazolium groups grafted CDs (ImCDs) participated into the proton conduction process through providing imidazolium groups for ameliorating proton conduction network. The enhanced proton conduction was achieved through constructing multilayered structure. Specifically, the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane exhibited the proton conductivity of 5.61 × 10-3 S/cm at 150 °C, which was higher than 7.73 × 10-4 S/cm of the PVC/CDs/PA membrane. Notably, the PA doped membrane could retain the mechanical strength without microstructure expansion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polyvinyl chloride nanofibers
Multilayered structure
Proton conduction
Proton exchange membrane

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the solution casting membranes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We give thanks to Zhao Yun, a research fellow at Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences for the beneficial discussion about the property of the membrane. We are grateful for the financial supports by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21703029) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2005001). Special thanks are due to the instrumental/data analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University.

We give thanks to Zhao Yun, a research fellow at Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences for the beneficial discussion about the property of the membrane. We are grateful for the financial supports by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21703029) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2005001). Special thanks are due to the instrumental/data analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematically represented the preparation process of the CDs-based composite membranes (A); the microstructure of the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane (B); the image of PVC/CDs and (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membranes (C).
FTIR (A) and XRD (B) of the prepared membranes.
Thermogravimetric analysis (A), oxidative stability (B) and component stability (C), (D) of the prepared CDs-based composite membranes.

SEM images of the fine microstructure on surface and cross section of the prepared membranes. The surface observation: (A) PVC; (B) PVC nanofibers; (C) PVC/CDs; (D) PVC/ImCDs; (E) (PVC/CDs/PVC)es; (F) PVC/CDs/PA; (G) PVC/ImCDs/PA; (H) (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA; The cross section observation: (I) PVC/CDs; (J) PVC/ImCDs; (K) (PVC/CDs/PVC)es; (L) PVC/CDs/PA; (M) and (N) PVC/ImCDs/PA; (O) and (P) (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA.
Methanol permeability of the CDs-based composite membranes during a 168 h non-stop test.
Weight gain and volume swelling of the PVC solution casting membrane, the PVC nanofibers membrane and the CDs-based composite membranes with PA doping.
Proton conductivities (A) and Arrhenius plots (B) of PA doped CDs-based electrospinning membrane and solution casting membranes.
The proposed proton conduction processes in (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA (A) and PVC/CDs (B) membranes.
The mechanical property of the prepared composite membranes based on PVC nanofibers (A) and PVC (B).

head.ce:title: Enhancing proton conduction of high temperature proton exchange membranes based on carbon dots doped polyvinyl chloride nanofibers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang 110819, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) as the easy-to-get and cheap carbon nanomaterials exhibited the great potential in various high-performance electrolytes. In this research, we constructed high temperature proton exchange membranes with multilayered microstructures through a couple of polyvinyl chloride (PVC) nanofibers layers wrapping a thin CDs layer. In the prepared (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane, the CDs provided a mount of sites to anchor phosphoric acid (PA) molecules with the formation of the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane. The proton conduction was accelerated by the continuous proton conduction channels consisting of the CDs layer and PA molecular chains. Notably, the proton conduction behavior was guided by the PVC nanofibers in the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane. Furthermore, a large number of hydrophilic oxygenated functional groups surrounding CDs facilitated the proton conduction process owing to the reduced proton conduction resistance in the hydrophilic membrane. For the PVC/ImCDs/PA membrane, the imidazolium groups could enhance proton conductivity. From our perspective, the imidazolium groups grafted CDs (ImCDs) participated into the proton conduction process through providing imidazolium groups for ameliorating proton conduction network. The enhanced proton conduction was achieved through constructing multilayered structure. Specifically, the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane exhibited the proton conductivity of 5.61 × 10-3 S/cm at 150 °C, which was higher than 7.73 × 10-4 S/cm of the PVC/CDs/PA membrane. Notably, the PA doped membrane could retain the mechanical strength without microstructure expansion.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fuel cells are considered to be the electrochemical devices that convert the chemical energy of fuels into electrical energy with the theoretical energy conversion efficiency close to 100% [1]. As one typical fuel cell, proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs) have become the research hotspot owing to the rapid start-up, high energy conversion efficiency, low price, negligible emission and wide range of applications, etc [2–4]. The high temperature PEMFCs with the operation temperature exceeding 110 °C could overcome some intrinsic problems, such as complex water/thermal management, slow electrochemical reaction rate and low tolerance to CO poison [5–9]. As the core component of PEMFCs, proton exchange membranes (PEMs) provided proton conduction channels and separated oxidants from fuels [10–12]. Thus, it was of great significance to perform the intensive research on the operation of the PEMs working in high temperature condition, which catered to the development of high temperature PEMFCs [13–15].

At current, the research of PA molecules as the anhydrous proton conduction carrier was relatively mature. Thus, high temperature proton exchange membranes (HTPEMs) based on PA doping polymers have been attracting more attentions [16–18]. As reported, PA molecules were captured by the functional groups in polymers, such as polybenzimidazole (PBI) [19], triazole-polysulfone [20] and aramid nanofibers [21] et al. For the polymers without basic sites to combine PA molecules, some additives of ionic liquids [22], inorganic nanocrystals [23], metal organic frameworks (MOFs) [24], functional polymers [25] and carbon nanomaterials including carbon dots [26], carbon nanotubes [27], graphene oxides [28] were introduced as bridges to link PA molecules and polymers. Polymerization ionic liquid (PIL) membranes were synthesized through the imidazolium cations of ionic liquids grafting the molecular chains of PVC. The fixed imidazolium cations combined PA molecules and provided proton conduction channels [29]. Subsequently, the membranes were prepared based on polyvinylpyrrolidone reacting with PVC. The crosslinker of alkaline amine-rich polyamidoamine could combine more PA molecules owing to high affinity [30].

The CDs as the zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial have the merits of easy preparation, wide sources of raw materials, high chemical inertness, low toxicity and low price, etc [31,32]. According to the carbon core and attached states, the CDs were primarily divided into four groups [33], such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), and polymer dots (PDs). Various hydrophilic stabilizers guaranteed the formation of hydrophilic CDs with the hydrophobic property of carbon core. The PEMs based on the pristine CDs and the modified CDs have been reported in succession [34–36]. He RH et al. reported that the CDs could alter the microphase separation of membranes and thus accelerated the proton conduction [26]. The results demonstrated the practicality of CDs in the preparation of HTPEMs. Wu PY et al. synthesized the Nafion modified CDs and reported the enhancement on proton conductivity and methanol resistance [37]. Kim JD et al. prepared the CNDs-based membrane and reported the proton conductivities of 56.3 mS/cm at 80 °C and 81.8 mS/cm at 120 °C [38]. Li H et al. fabricated the CDs-based membrane with the improved chemical stability. The unsaturated groups reacting with the active free radicals could benefit high retention of proton conductivity and cell performance [39]. Furthermore, the proton conduction at subzero temperature was investigated and the proton conductivities of the (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200/PA membrane reached (2.99 ± 0.39) × 10-2 S/cm at 30 °C and (3.29 ± 3.16) × 10-3 S/cm at −30 °C [40].

In this research, the CDs-based PEMs were respectively prepared with electrospinning technique and solution casting technique. The electrospinning technology was believed to be an facile method to prepare membranes composed of the polymer fibers with diameters from submicron to nanometer [41]. In the electrospinning membrane, a thin CDs layer was wrapped by a couple of PVC electrospinning nanofibers layers to form sandwich-like structure. For the prepared (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane, the well-ordered microstructure was considered to reduce the proton conduction resistance. Furthermore, the multilayered membrane was prepared without the process of mixing PVC and CDs, which could avoid the aggregation of the CDs during the membrane preparation process. The outer PVC nanofibers prevented the leakage of the CDs and confined the proton conduction. Although the nanofiber network structure of PVC could accommodate PA molecules, the CDs layer dominated the combination of PA molecules with the formed intermolecular hydrogen bonds between oxygen-containing groups of hydroxyl, epoxy, carboxyl and PA molecules. In the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane, CDs provided continuous proton conduction channels and PVC nanofibers reduced proton conduction resistance through regulating protons permeation. Furthermore, the introduction of imidazolium groups could enhance the proton conductivity of the solution casting membrane. Most importantly, the improved chemical stability and the strengthened mechanical property were derived from the chemically inert and compact structure of the outer PVC nanofibers layers.

Polyvinyl chloride (molecular weight: 58000–63000), N-methylimidazole (NMI), ethylenediamine and polyphosphoric acid (PPA) were received from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Citric acid was purchased from Tianjin Yongda Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) was obtained from Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. The 100 wt% PA was prepared through dissolving 125.2 g of PPA into 20 mL of deionized (DI) water.

Carboxylated CDs surrounding with hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups were synthesized with the previous reports [37,42]. In brief, the light brown CDs solution was obtained through 2,6-diaminopyridine reacting with citric acid in the hydrothermal process at 200 °C for 4 h. More details about the synthesization of CDs were provided in the Supplementary Information (SI).

Approximate 12 wt% of PVC/DMF solution was prepared through dissolving 1.20 g of PVC powder into 9.28 mL (Weight: 8.80 g, Density: 0.948 g/mL) of DMF. The PVC/DMF solution (6.0 mL) was electrospun on a piece of aluminum foil as the substrate with the flow rate of 0.6 mL/h, voltage of 19.22 kV, rotation speed of 180 rpm, temperature of 23 °C and relative humidity (RH) of 35%. The 1 wt% of CDs aqueous solution (1.0 mL) was sprayed on the surface of the PVC nanofibers layer and heated at 35 °C for 1 h. A thin PVC/CDs layer was formed after repeating the CDs deposition process for 5 times. Subsequently, 6.0 mL of PVC/DMF solution was electrospun on the PVC/CDs layer and heated at 35 °C overnight. The free-standing (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane was obtained after gently peeling off the substrate. According to the weight variation of the membranes during the preparation process, the weight ratio of PVC/CDs was 6/1 in the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the solution casting membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The PVC/CDs membrane with the weight ratio of 6/1 was fabricated with solution casting method and the details about the membrane preparation were shown in the SI. The PVC/ImCDs membrane was fabricated with immersing the PVC/CDs membrane in the 5 M of NMI/DMF solution at 80 °C for 48 h. Furthermore, the CDs deposit was mixed with the equal weight of 100 wt% PA and the mixture of CDs-PA was added into the PVC/DMF solution. The PVC/CDs-PA membrane was prepared using the homogeneous solution with the solution casting method.

The pristine PVC nanofibers membrane and composite membranes were immersed in 100 wt% PA at 60 °C for 24 h to fabricate PA doped membranes, such as the PVC-NFs/PA, (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA, PVC/CDs/PA, PVC/ImCDs/PA and PVC/CDs-PA/PA.

The structure and property of the prepared membranes were characterized with dynamic light scattering (DLS), fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), thermal/oxidative/components stabilities, scanning electron microscope (SEM), methanol permeability, weight gain, dimension swelling, proton conductivity, mechanical property and fuel cell performance. More details about equipments, operation processes and test conditions were described in the SI.

The prepared CDs deposits were hydrophilic owing to the oxygen-containing groups of hydroxyl, epoxy, carboxyl, which facilitated to form the homogeneous CDs solution. According to the DLS results, the diameter of the CDs was 4.5 ± 2.2 nm statistically. The zeta potential (ξ) of −24.7 mV was indicative of the CDs bearing many negative charges on the surface. Ronzani C et al. reported the cationic CDs bearing the amine group-containing passivation reagents with the ξ values in the range of (11.2–37.3) mV [43].

Fig. 1 (A) schematically illustrated the preparation process of the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane and the sandwich-like structure of a dual PVC nanofibers layers wrapping the inner CDs layer (Fig. 1 (B)). The PVC nanofibers possessed the average diameters of (55 ± 20) nm and the length of more than 200 μm statistically. From the SEM image in Fig. 4 (K), a portion of CDs embedded in the outer PVC nanofibers in the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane. The thicknesses of the PVC nanofibers layer and the CDs layer were respectively (17.2 ± 2.8) μm and (14.8 ± 2.3) μm. Although higher content of CDs was favor of the proton conduction, the mechanical strength would be dragged for (PVC/CDs/PVC)es and (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membranes. The almost identical thickness of the CDs and the out PVC nanofibers layer was satisfactory. Owing to intermolecular hydrogen bonds, a large number of PA molecules were stable in the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane. Because the PVC nanofibers mechanically supported composite membranes, the free-standing CDs-based composite membranes exhibited the good balance on flexibility and stiffness (Fig. 1 (C)). According to the micrometer measurement, the thickness values of (PVC/CDs/PVC)es and (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membranes were respectively 39 ± 2 μm and 41 ± 2 μm, reminiscent of Nafion-112 with 50 μm and Nafion-1135 with 88 μm [44].

From the FTIR spectra in Fig. 2 (A), the PVC nanofibers exhibited the peak at 613 cm−1 responding to the C-Cl group [45]. For the CDs, some tiny peaks at 3406 cm−1, 1662 cm−1, 1548 cm−1, 1387 cm−1, 1180 cm−1 and 1049 cm−1 were assigned to –OH, carboxyl C = O, N–H, -C = C, C-N-C and epoxy C-O groups. Notably, the mentioned peaks were observed in the prepared CDs-based composite membranes, albeit with some variations on intensity and position. The characteristic peaks at 1520 cm−1 and 742 cm−1 accounting for imidazolium groups were indicative of the successful introduction of imidazolium cations from the ImCDs in the PVC/ImCDs membrane.

The XRD profile in Fig. 2 (B) represented the wide peaks centering at 2θ = 18.0°, indicating the amorphous microstructure of PVC nanofibers. Furthermore, there was an imperceptible variation on the 2θ value of the primary peak in the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane. According to the Bragg's equation (Eq. S9), the result meant that the CDs layer was adhered to PVC nanofibers without expanding the polymer skeleton. For PA doped membranes, the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane maintained the stable microstructure without PA molecules swelling polymer skeleton, deriving from the invariable 2θ value of the peak. As reported, the reported lower shift of phosphosilicate doping PBI [46] and higher shift of PA molecules doping (PU/Nafion/PU/CDs)200 membrane [40] meant the variation on microstructure. From the results, it was believed that PA molecules were contained in the gaps, deriving from the accumulation of PVC nanofibers.

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), oxidative stability and component stability were measured to evaluate the stability of the prepared membranes. For the thermal stability, the Td (5%), defining as the decomposition temperature of the 5% weight, is an index to estimate the initial thermal decomposition [47]. From Fig. 3
(A), the CDs, the PVC nanofibers membrane and the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane respectively represented the Td (5%) values of 185 °C, 270 °C and 180 °C. For the polymer of PVC, Liu GJ et al. reported a multi-stage process including the first stage of thermal degradation in the temperature interval of 238–409 °C and the second stage in the range of 409–543 °C [48]. Notably, the pristine PVC nanofibers membrane and the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane could maintain the fibrous structure (Figure S1) even if they were heated at 150 °C for 24 h. For the PVC/ImCDs membrane, the additional quick weight loss period of 190–260 °C was primarily caused by continuous decomposition of the disubstituted imidazole cations [49].

The chemical stability of the membrane was estimated on the basis of morphology variation and weight loss with the sample immersing in Fenton reagent at 68 °C for 164 h. From Fig. 3 (B), the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane possessed the integrity on the appearance with a residual weight (RW) percent of 96.6% at 48 h. Some cracks and even fractures were observed with a RW percent of 75.9% at 164 h. The fine oxidative stability was attributed to the outer PVC nanofibers layers protecting the inner CDs layer from the strong oxidizers, such as HO·, HOO·, Fe3+ and O2. Furthermore, PVC/CDs, PVC/ImCDs and PVC/CDs-PA membranes respectively maintained the RW percents of 81.7%, 102.5% and 102.8%. Some water molecules doping by ImCDs and CDs-PA led to higher RW values. As reported, the RW percent was near 90% of the cross-linked PBI membrane in Fenton reagent for 144 h at 68 °C [50]. As regards to the Nafion® 111 membrane, the structural defect was mentioned while it was stored in Fenton reagent for 48 h [51].

Fig. 3 (C) and (D) exhibited the weight variation trend of (PVC/CDs/PVC)es and (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membranes with full humidification at 100 °C. In particular, there was a slight variation on weight of the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane. For the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane, the weight drop stage within the initial 32 h was caused by the leakage of PA molecules. Furthermore, (PVC/CDs/PVC)es and (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membranes maintained the RW percents of 99.3% and 70.3% for 183 h. The PA/PTFE/PP-29%HP membrane was reported to possess the PA retention of 54.3% at 100 °C and 30% RH [30]. Lu SF et al reported that no leaching of PA from the blend membrane based on polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) was detected during 10 days of operation [25].

For the PEMs, the microstructure could affect and even dominate the overall performance. From Fig. 4 (A), the pristine PVC membrane exhibited compact microstructure, albeit with some clusters on the surface. By comparison, the interconnected network consisting of PVC nanofibers was observed in the electrospinning nanofibers membrane (Fig. 4 (B)). Some clusters, deriving from the aggregation of CDs were observed on the surface of the PVC/CDs membrane because of the microstructure separation of hydrophilic CDs with hydrophobic PVC. The AFM image of CDs (Figure S2) demonstrated the aggregation of CDs. Notably, some micropores with the diameter of (1.1 ± 0.35) μm were observed on the surface of the PVC/ImCDs membrane (Fig. 4 (D)). Furthermore, the micropores were enlarged because PA molecules expanded the polymer skeleton of PVC/CDs/PA (Fig. 4 (F)) and PVC/ImCDs/PA (Fig. 4 (G)) membranes. By comparison, some clusters due to the aggregation of CDs were observed on the surface of the (PVC/CDs)es membrane (Figure S3). Wang JT et al. mentioned the aggregation of the CDs in the polymer of polyethyleneimine (PEI). The good compatibility between CDs and PEI chains could enable a complete active layer [52]. From Fig. 4 (E) and (H), the electrospining membranes of (PVC/CDs/PVC)es and (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA exhibited the filamentary microstructures.

In the view of cross section, PVC/CDs (Fig. 4 (I)) and PVC/ImCDs (Fig. 4 (J)) membranes showed compact microstructures. Notably, the invariable microstructures were observed even if PA molecules were doped in PVC/CDs/PA (Fig. 4 (L)) and PVC/ImCDs/PA (Fig. 4 (M) and (N)) membranes. Although there was a crack between the PVC nanofibers layer and the CDs layer, the dense microstructure was reckoned to form in (PVC/CDs/PVC)es (Fig. 4 (K)) and (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA (Fig. 4 (O) and (P)) membranes. Furthermore, the element dispersion of the membranes (Figure S4-S10) verified the well-ordered dispersion of components in membranes.

The proton conduction channels were reported to facilitate methanol molecules permeation, which hindered the commercialization of PEMs in direct methanol fuel cells (DMFCs) [53]. There was the concern of introducing the hydrophilic CDs into the CDs-based composite membrane would exert negative effect on the resistance to methanol molecules permeation. From the methanol permeability in Fig. 5 , a range of methanol permeability (p) values were presented because of the variations on chemical potential and concentration during the test process, which has been discussed in our previous research [54]. Specifically, the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane exhibited the p values of (0.82–1.42) × 10-6 cm2/s with 2 M methanol solution as source within 168 h. By comparison, PVC/CDs and PVC/ImCDs membranes respectively represented the p values of (0.62–2.62) × 10-6 cm2/s and (1.39–1.83) × 10-6 cm2/s. Kundu PP et al. reported the Nafion-117 membrane with the p value of 1.2 × 10-6 cm2/s with 2 M methanol solution [55]. Notably, there was an obvious fluctuation on p values of PVC/CDs, which was probably caused by the leakage of hydrophilic CDs. From the stable p values, it was inferred that the outer PVC nanofibers could alleviate the leakage of CDs from the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membrane.

ce:section.ce:label: 3.7 Weight
ce:section.ce:para: For PA doped membranes, we believed that PA molecules dominated the proton conduction accompanying with the well-ordered microstructure reducing proton conduction resistance. The PEMs with a suitable content of PA and a lower dimension swelling would be more competitive owing to the simultaneous enhancement on proton conductivity and mechanical property. From Fig. 6 , the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane showed a higher weight gain (WG) of 85.6% and a volume swelling (VS) of 4.28% relative to 20.5% and 2.83% of the PVC/CDs/PA membrane. The combination of PA molecules followed the same mechanism. Specifically, PA molecules were combined with intermolecular hydrogen bonds between PA molecules and oxygen-containing groups of hydroxyl, epoxy and carboxyl on the surface of CDs. Subsequently, more PA molecules were captured with the intermolecular hydrogen bonds among PA molecules. Although there was imperceptible weight variation with immersing the PVC solution membrane in 100 wt% PA, the WG value reached 13.6% for the PVC nanofibers membrane. On the basis of the results, the PVC nanofibers membrane accommodated PA molecules owing to the interlaced nanofibers and stratified structure without microstructure expansion. Relative to the PVC/CDs/PA membrane, the PVC/ImCDs/PA membrane possessed a higher WG value of 42.0 % since the grafted imidazolium groups provided more sites to combine PA molecules.

The proton conductivity (σ) and Arrhenius plots of the prepared PA doped CDs-based composite membranes were shown in Fig. 7 (A) and (B). For the solution casting membrane, the abnormal phenomenon of the σ decreasing with temperature rising was observed in the PVC/CDs/PA membrane, which was primarily caused by the accelerated components leakage at the elevated temperature. The trend of hydrophilic carbon nanomaterials leaking from hydrophobic polymers was reported due to the lack of strong interaction in composite membranes [56,57]. Therefore, the PVC/CDs-PA/PA membrane exhibited the lower σ value of 7.73 × 10-4 S/cm at 150 °C. By comparison, the enhanced σ values were observed in (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA and PVC/ImCDs/PA membranes, such as 5.61 × 10-3 S/cm and 6.79 × 10-3 S/cm at 150 °C. The results were indicative of the stable existence of CDs in the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane, deriving from the protection of the outer PVC nanofibers layers in the electrospinning membrane. The enhanced σ values of the PVC/ImCDs/PA membrane were attributed to the formation of proton conduction channels consisting of the imidazolium groups in ImCDs. Wu PY et al. reported Nafion/0.5-CD membrane with the σ value of near 0.01 S/cm [37]. A highest conductivity of 0.086 S/cm at 180 °C was reported by He RH et al. in the composite membrane of CDs doped polyethersulfone [26]. Recently, Kim TH et al. prepared the Nafion membrane coated with N-doped CDs with a conductivity of 240.3 mS/cm at 80 °C [58]. Furthermore, there was the possibility of hydrophobicity decrease due to hydrophilic imidazolium groups grafting to the molecular chains of PVC nanofibers, which could contribute to the combination of ImCDs in the PVC/ImCDs/PA membrane.

For PA doped membranes, the proton conduction primarily obeyed the matrix transport with vehicular mechanism. On the basis of the Arrhenius plots (Fig. 7 (B)), we calculated the activation energy (Ea) values in the range of 80–150 °C, such as 19.6 kJ/mol of (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA and 26.5 kJ/mol of PVC/ImCDs/PA. The data were comparable to 19.7 kJ·mol−1 of SPEEK [59] and 21.5 kJ/mol of the organic–inorganic membrane of CeTPT (1:2) [60], higher than 15.2 kJ/mol of (Kevlar/CdTe)25/(PU/CdTe)100/PA [61] and 13.43 kJ/mol of SPEEK/NIM-SiO2 [62]. In general, a lower Ea value identified the reduced proton conduction resistance, which can be a sign of well-interconnected proton conduction channels in PEMs.

For PA doped composite membranes, it was believed that the well-ordered dispersion of components could promote proton conduction with the reduced resistance [63]. Fig. 8 schematically described the proposed proton conduction process in the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane. The continuous proton conduction channels were derived from the formed intermolecular hydrogen bonds between PA molecules and oxygenated functional groups surrounding the CDs. For the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane, the accelerating speed of proton conductivity with temperature rising was slow down exceeding 120 °C. The insufficient proton conduction carriers due to a lower weight gain of PA molecules was the primary reason. Protons transferred with the fast motion of PA molecules in the inherent proton conduction network. Furthermore, the outer PVC nanofibers confined the proton motion pathways besides supported the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane.

The mechanical properties of the membranes including tensile stress (E) and elongation (ε) were shown in Fig. 9 . From Fig. 9 (A), the E values of the PVC-NFs and (PVC/CDs/PVC)es membranes were respectively 3.66 MPa and 3.08 MPa. The similar E values were indicative of the stable combination of CDs with PVC nanofibers. Olekhnovich RO et al. reported that the tensile stress of the PVC nanofiber mats with the rotation speed of 500 rpm reached (3.0 ± 0.3) MPa [64]. The ANMs-C2H5-X/PVCcomposite film possessed the similar E value relative to that of the pristine PVC nanofibers [65]. Notably, a slight decrease on the E value occurred with PA molecules doping, such as 2.45 MPa of the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane. From our perspective, most of PA molecules entered into the gaps between PVC nanofibers without microstructure expansion of the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane. Furthermore, the strength of the membranes would be enhanced with the CDs. Li H et al. reported that the E values increased from 41.68 MPa to 46.2 MPa while HA-CQDs was incorporated to the PFSA membrane [39]. From Fig. 9 (B), PVC/CDs/PA and PVC/ImCDs/PA membranes exhibited the reduced E values of 15.1 MPa and 11.1 MPa, which was caused by the breakdown of Van der Waals force between the molecular chains of PVC resulted in the reduction of E values, such as 15.1 MPa and 11.1 MPa (Fig. 9 (B)). The results were comparable with 17.1 MPa of SFPEEKK-60/NCC-10 [66] and 9.7 MPa of semi-IPN-1.0/0.5OPBI/PA [67]. Furthermore, the repared PVC nanofibers based composite membranes exhibited the similar ε values in the range of 2.2%-3.1%, which was indicative of the invariable elasticity.

The fuel cell test based on the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane at 100 °C was performed with anhydrous hydrogen and oxygen. The open circuit voltage (OCV) was near 0.60 V, which was similar with 0.54 V of the GQD + HBM membrane [68], lower than the reported OCV values, such as 0.98 V of the PVDF–PVP80 composite membrane [25], 0.97 V of the PP-2CD36.2/10CD93.1 membrane [26] and 0.95 V of the PCNT + PGO/RN membrane [27]. The lower OCV was primarily caused by the with higher gas permeability due to the fibrous structure. Additionally, the performance of the fuel cell based on the (PVC/CDs/PVC)es/PA membrane was mediocre because the electrodes are commercial without optimization.

This research proposed the strategy of enhancing the proton conduction of high temperature proton exchange membranes through constructing the sandwich-like microstructure. A couple of PVC nanofibers layers wrapped a thin CDs layer with the electrospinning technique. Besides the PVC nanofibers accommodated PA molecules, more PA molecules were anchored by the CDs layer through intermolecular hydrogen bonds. The proton conduction was accelerated by continuous proton conduction channels in the CDs layer and guided by the PVC electrospining nanofibers. The results could demonstrate that the electrospinning membrane possessed fine stability and enhanced proton conductivity. The improved chemical stability and the strengthened mechanical property were derived from the chemically inert and compact structure of the outer PVC nanofibers layers. Further research would be focused on constructing high temperature proton exchange membranes with other carbon nanomaterials and electrospinning nanofibers, expecting to determine the university of the electrospinning technique to prepare high performance membrane electrolytes.

Ke Liu: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology. Xiaoqing Wei: Investigation, Data curation. Shu Hu: Investigation. Qingquan Li: Investigation. Weimin Gao: Investigation. Dan Wu: Investigation. Quantong Che: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We give thanks to Zhao Yun, a research fellow at Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences for the beneficial discussion about the property of the membrane. We are grateful for the financial supports by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21703029) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (N2005001). Special thanks are due to the instrumental/data analysis from Analytical and Testing Center, Northeastern University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2023.124679.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
